author: @FrancisRodgers
text: I saw the concept with the rotating piston in part 3. Very interesting. But can you perhaps add an outer coil (or coil of magnets or whatever) to this that this inner coil will move through, and in doing so induce a current. This would reduce friction caused by pistons and overcoming initial motor inertia because you can generate more electricity out than you are putting in which you could use to drive a motor or other such thing directly. Perhaps to even reduce things further you may consider adding a simple switch on either end which when hit reverses the polarity. No need for complicated switching mechanisms and then the system can go as fast as it needs to. Finally, perhaps putting small and weak springs on either end so that the energy absorbed by the impact at either end can be used to push the inner coil making it less work for the system to do it all by itself. If you really want to get fancy, you could instead replace the inner most steel bar with some magnets of sort and have it somehow magnetically levitated reducing friction even more (but that's probably over complicating things a bit). Maybe most of this wont work but I just taught I'd share my crazy ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: give it a go and see

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Interesting idea - mostly this little thing is a wonder of nature kind of experiment bit like the SMOTs you see everywhere. I'm not sure on it's scalability or even its practicality - still i though it was a fascinating thing and as a springboard to something else very interesting. If you go with your ideas let me know I'd love to see how they turn out.

author: @vidbid1
text: What about using your device to run a piston type of engine, e.g., single stroke motor. You could also harvest the back emf, store it, and dump it back to the motor.

author: @gotoluc
text:
Hi Robert, great replications.

However, if you want the flux of the opposite side of the magnet to reach the outside of the enclosure the cores will have to be reduced. Also, the outside core enclosure has to be as close to the outside coil windings as possible to make a difference.

All the best

Luc

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hi - I checked out the vids and the thread on overunity - it really annoys me when people decry something without trying it - i always think theory is just a lack of good practice - it seems to completely escape some people that the solenoid is incapable of doing any work until you add your magnet arrangement and suddenly work is being done without an increase in power used. Who cares if the theory says one thing when we can see it working!!

author: @MikePoirier
text: Good work Robert. I really enjoy this. This week, I was just discussing the idea with Gotoluc. I have replicated the basic principle, see video "Mostly Permanent Magnet Experiment" I'm looking towards more development. Thanks for your interest. Your audio cutting in and out Robert. Cheers.
